maturity.advanced: Technical data labelling by purpose in data platform; automated enforcement preventing data flows outside permitted purpose boundaries; purpose limitation metrics tracked in governance dashboard; privacy by design gate for new data use cases requires DPO sign-off on purpose compatibility

toolExamples: Data catalogues with purpose tagging (Collibra, Alation)
API gateway policies for purpose-based access
controls.controlObjective: Ensure personal data collected for a specified purpose is not processed in a manner incompatible with that purpose, aligned with the General Principle (s6) and Disclosure Principle (s8), protecting data subjects from unexpected secondary uses of their data.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Permitted purposes for each data category are documented in the ROPA and consent records. Technical access controls prevent use of personal data for non-authorised purposes. Purpose compatibility assessments are conducted before any new use of existing personal data. Staff training covers purpose limitation obligations. Data processing agreements include purpose limitation requirements.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Using personal data for purposes beyond what was consented to violates s6 and s8. Data subjects lose trust when data is used unexpectedly. Commissioner may issue enforcement notice. Purpose creep is a common audit finding that demonstrates governance weakness.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the ROPA for documented permitted purposes per data category. Sample 5 processing activities and verify they fall within the documented purposes. Request evidence of purpose compatibility assessments for any new uses of existing data in the last 12 months. Review 3 data processing agreements for purpose limitation clauses.

controls.name: Data Breach Detection and Triage
controls.description: Implement controls for the timely detection of personal data breaches from multiple sources (security alerts, employee reports, third-party notifications, vulnerability scans) and a rapid triage process to assess notifiability under PDPA s12B within the 72-hour window
keyActivities: Establish and publicise multiple breach reporting channels (IT helpdesk, DPO email, security hotline)
Define and train staff on personal data breach indicators and reporting obligations
Implement automated security monitoring with breach-specific alert rules (see data-security domain)
Conduct initial triage within 4 hours of breach report: confirm, classify, and assess notifiability
Document triage outcomes including decision not to notify with rationale

maturity.basic: Staff instructed to report suspected breaches to IT and DPO; IT investigates and escalates; checklist for initial triage
maturity.mature: Dedicated breach reporting inbox with auto-acknowledgement; breach triage template with severity matrix; defined escalation path to DPO and CISO; 72-hour countdown starts on confirmation; breach register updated at triage
maturity.advanced: 24/7 breach detection through SOC integration; automated initial classification of security events as potential breaches; breach management platform tracking triage, notification, and remediation; regulatory countdown timer with automated escalation; API integration with Commissioner portal for notification

banking.additionalRequirements: Dual reporting to Commissioner under s12B and to BNM per incident reporting requirements

insurance.additionalRequirements: Dual reporting to Commissioner and BNM for significant security incidents

aviation.additionalRequirements: Notify both Commissioner and CAAM for significant passenger data breaches

controls.controlObjective: Enable timely detection of personal data breaches from multiple sources and rapid triage to assess notifiability under s12B within the 72-hour window, ensuring the organization can meet its mandatory notification obligation.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Multiple breach reporting channels are established and publicised (IT helpdesk, DPO email, security hotline). Staff are trained on breach indicators and reporting obligations. Automated security monitoring includes breach-specific alert rules. Initial triage is conducted within 4 hours of breach report: confirm, classify, assess notifiability. Triage outcomes including decisions not to notify are documented with rationale.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Late detection means the 72-hour notification clock under s12B is compressed or missed. Failure to notify a notifiable breach carries a fine of up to RM500,000 under s12B. Undetected breaches cause ongoing harm to data subjects. Commissioner may view poor detection capability as evidence of s9 non-compliance.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the breach register for the last 24 months. For all recorded breaches, verify triage was completed within 4 hours and notifiability assessment documented. Review breach reporting channels and verify they are published in training materials. Test staff awareness by reviewing the most recent security awareness training content for breach reporting coverage.

controls.name: Mandatory Breach Notification to Commissioner
controls.description: Implement a process to ensure mandatory notification of notifiable personal data breaches to the Personal Data Protection Commissioner within 72 hours of becoming aware, as required by PDPA s12B inserted by Amendment Act A1727, with all prescribed notification content
keyActivities: Determine notifiability: assess whether breach is likely to result in harm to data subjects
Prepare notification using Commissioner's prescribed format including all mandatory content
Submit notification to Commissioner within 72 hours of becoming aware of the notifiable breach
Submit supplementary notification if full information is not available at initial notification
Retain copies of all notifications, submissions, and Commissioner correspondence

maturity.basic: DPO prepares notification manually from Commissioner guidance; submitted via email or portal within 72 hours; copy filed in DPO records
maturity.mature: Breach notification template pre-drafted against s12B requirements; notifiability assessment checklist; DPO and CISO jointly sign off on notification; legal review for significant breaches; timeline log documenting notification steps
maturity.advanced: Automated notification drafting from breach management platform data fields; real-time 72-hour countdown with alerts; legal and DPO review workflow integrated in platform; direct API submission to Commissioner portal where available; automated supplementary notification scheduling

toolExamples: Breach notification templates aligned with s12B

banking.additionalRequirements: Dual reporting to Commissioner under s12B and to BNM per incident reporting requirements

insurance.additionalRequirements: Dual reporting to Commissioner and BNM for significant security incidents

aviation.additionalRequirements: Notify both Commissioner and CAAM for significant passenger data breaches

controls.controlObjective: Ensure mandatory notification of notifiable personal data breaches to the Commissioner within 72 hours of becoming aware, as required by s12B (inserted by A1727), with all prescribed notification content, to comply with the highest-penalty obligation in the PDPA.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Notifiability is assessed against s12B harm criteria. Notification is prepared using the Commissioner's prescribed format with all mandatory content. Notification is submitted within 72 hours of becoming aware. Supplementary notifications are submitted when full information becomes available. Copies of all notifications, submissions, and Commissioner correspondence are retained.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Failure to notify a notifiable breach within 72 hours carries the highest penalty in the PDPA: fine up to RM500,000 and/or imprisonment up to 3 years under s12B. Commissioner may publicize non-compliance. Reputational damage is compounded by the appearance of a cover-up.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review all breach notifications submitted to the Commissioner in the last 24 months. For each, verify submission within 72 hours, prescribed content completeness, and supplementary notification where applicable. Review 3 breaches assessed as non-notifiable and verify the rationale documentation is robust.

controls.name: Breach Containment and Remediation
controls.description: Implement documented containment and remediation procedures for personal data breaches by breach type, ensuring the breach is stopped, the root cause is identified and addressed, and recurrence is prevented, as part of the overall breach response obligation under PDPA s12B and s9
keyActivities: Execute breach-type-specific containment playbooks (ransomware, unauthorized access, data loss, insider threat)
Preserve evidence for forensic investigation before containment actions where possible
Conduct root cause analysis to identify the technical, process, or human failure that enabled the breach
Develop and implement remediation action plan addressing identified root causes
Conduct post-incident review within 30 days and implement lessons learned

maturity.basic: IT team contains breach by isolating affected systems; DPO coordinates with management; remediation actions assigned in follow-up meeting; incident report filed
maturity.mature: Written incident response playbooks by breach type; forensic evidence preservation procedure; root cause analysis template; remediation action plan tracked to completion; post-incident review report shared with management
maturity.advanced: Cyber incident response retainer with specialist forensic firm; SOAR platform for automated containment actions (account lockout, network isolation); real-time threat intelligence integration during response; tabletop exercises twice yearly; breach remediation effectiveness metrics

controls.controlObjective: Stop active breaches, identify root causes, and prevent recurrence through documented containment and remediation procedures, fulfilling the ongoing security obligation under s9 and supporting the breach response framework under s12B.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Breach-type-specific containment playbooks exist (ransomware, unauthorized access, data loss, insider threat). Evidence is preserved for forensic investigation before containment actions where possible. Root cause analysis identifies the technical, process, or human failure that enabled the breach. A remediation action plan addresses identified root causes. Post-incident review is completed within 30 days with lessons learned implemented.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Without containment, breaches escalate causing greater harm and regulatory exposure. Without root cause analysis, breaches recur. Repeated breaches of the same type demonstrate systemic s9 failure. Commissioner may view inadequate remediation as aggravating factor in enforcement.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review all breach incidents from the last 24 months. For each, verify containment actions were taken, root cause analysis completed, remediation plan documented and tracked to closure, and post-incident review completed within 30 days. Review 2 containment playbooks for completeness against common breach scenarios.

controls.name: Data Subject Breach Notification
controls.description: Implement a process for notifying affected data subjects of a personal data breach where the breach is likely to result in harm to the data subjects, in compliance with PDPA s12B, including notification content, delivery methods, and timeline requirements
keyActivities: Assess harm likelihood and necessity of data subject notification based on breach nature and data type
Draft notification in plain language explaining the breach, data affected, potential harm, and protective steps
Select appropriate notification channel (email, letter, SMS, in-app, website notice) based on affected population
Deliver notification without undue delay once decision to notify is made
Establish dedicated helpline or contact point for affected data subjects

maturity.basic: DPO drafts notification letter; sent by email or post to affected data subjects; generic template adapted per incident
maturity.mature: Harm assessment framework for notification decisions; notification template library by channel; bulk email/SMS capability for large-scale breaches; dedicated helpline established for significant breaches; notification sent within 5 business days of notification decision
maturity.advanced: Dynamic notification platform with personalised content by affected data type; multi-channel delivery with delivery confirmation; credit monitoring or identity protection service offered for high-risk breaches; call centre script and FAQ prepared; regulatory-compliant notification content reviewed by legal and DPO

toolExamples: Email marketing platforms for bulk notification
controls.controlObjective: Notify affected data subjects of personal data breaches likely to result in harm, as required by s12B, with clear information about the breach, data affected, potential harm, and protective steps they should take.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Harm likelihood assessment determines the necessity of data subject notification. Notifications are drafted in plain language explaining the breach, data affected, potential harm, and protective steps. Appropriate notification channels are selected based on the affected population (email, letter, SMS, in-app, website). Notification is delivered without undue delay. A dedicated helpline or contact point is established for affected data subjects.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Failure to notify affected data subjects denies them the opportunity to take protective action (change passwords, monitor accounts, freeze credit). Commissioner may direct notification and publicize the failure. Data subjects who suffer harm without notification may pursue legal remedies. Severe reputational damage.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review all data subject breach notifications issued in the last 24 months. For each, verify harm assessment was documented, notification content included all required elements, delivery was timely, and a contact point was established. For breaches where data subject notification was not made, review the rationale documentation.

controls.name: Cross-Border Transfer Identification and Mapping
controls.description: Identify and map all flows of personal data outside Malaysia including transfers to cloud services, SaaS platforms, offshore processors, group companies, and business partners, to ensure all cross-border transfers are subject to appropriate safeguards under PDPA s129
keyActivities: Conduct data flow mapping to identify all cross-border transfers (including cloud, SaaS, and email)
Classify transfers by recipient country, recipient type, and data categories transferred
Record all identified transfers in the cross-border transfer register
Review transfer register quarterly for new or changed transfers
Assess whether any identified transfers lack adequate transfer mechanisms and prioritise remediation

maturity.basic: Manual data flow mapping exercise conducted annually by DPO; spreadsheet of known international transfers; ad hoc review when new vendors are onboarded
maturity.mature: Data flow mapping integrated with vendor onboarding process; cross-border transfer register maintained in ROPA tool; quarterly review of transfer register; IT procurement required to declare international transfers at contract stage
maturity.advanced: Automated data flow discovery tool scanning network traffic for international data flows; integration with cloud access security broker (CASB) to detect unsanctioned cross-border SaaS usage; real-time transfer monitoring dashboard; automated alerts for new or unregistered international data flows

aviation.additionalRequirements: PNR/API transfers to foreign authorities require documentation under s129; adequacy or derogation basis required

healthcare.additionalRequirements: Overseas telehealth providers must have s129-compliant transfer mechanisms

controls.controlObjective: Identify and map all flows of personal data outside Malaysia — including transfers to cloud services, SaaS platforms, offshore processors, group companies, and business partners — to ensure all cross-border transfers are subject to appropriate safeguards under s129.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: A comprehensive data flow mapping exercise identifies all cross-border transfers including cloud, SaaS, and email. Transfers are classified by recipient country, recipient type, and data categories. All identified transfers are recorded in a cross-border transfer register. The register is reviewed quarterly for new or changed transfers. Transfers lacking adequate transfer mechanisms are identified and prioritised for remediation.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Unknown cross-border transfers operate without safeguards, violating s129. Commissioner may impose transfer restrictions. Cloud and SaaS usage often involves cross-border transfers that go undetected without mapping. Non-compliance with s129 carries penalty exposure up to RM300,000 and/or 2 years imprisonment.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the cross-border transfer register for completeness. Compare against the ROPA and vendor register to identify any unmapped transfers. Sample 10 vendors and verify whether they transfer data outside Malaysia. For cloud/SaaS services, verify data residency and any cross-border transfer disclosure. Confirm quarterly review evidence for the last 4 quarters.

controls.name: Transfer Mechanism Selection and Implementation
controls.description: Select and implement an appropriate transfer mechanism for each cross-border transfer of personal data under PDPA s129, including adequacy determination, standard contractual clauses, binding corporate rules, explicit consent, or certification schemes, and document the selection rationale
keyActivities: Determine whether the recipient country has an adequacy determination from the Malaysian Commissioner
Where no adequacy exists, select appropriate alternative mechanism (SCCs, BCRs, consent, certification)
Execute standard contractual clauses or other contractual safeguards before initiating transfer
Obtain DPO approval for each transfer mechanism selection and document rationale
Register transfer mechanisms with the Commissioner where required under s129

maturity.basic: DPO manually checks approved country list before authorising transfers; SCCs executed for major non-approved country transfers; limited documentation of mechanism selection rationale
maturity.mature: Transfer mechanism decision tree approved by DPO and legal; SCCs template library for different transfer scenarios; mechanism selection documented in transfer register; Commissioner notification completed for applicable transfers; annual review of mechanism adequacy
maturity.advanced: Automated adequacy assessment against current approved country list; SCC execution workflow integrated with vendor contracting; BCR program management for intra-group transfers; mechanism currency monitoring for regulatory changes (adequacy decisions, new SCCs); transfer mechanism compliance dashboard

toolExamples: OneTrust Vendor Risk for transfer mechanism management

aviation.additionalRequirements: PNR/API transfers to foreign authorities require documentation under s129; adequacy or derogation basis required

healthcare.additionalRequirements: Overseas telehealth providers must have s129-compliant transfer mechanisms

controls.controlObjective: Select and implement an appropriate transfer mechanism for each cross-border transfer under s129 — including adequacy determination, standard contractual clauses, binding corporate rules, or explicit consent — and document the selection rationale.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Each cross-border transfer has an identified transfer mechanism (adequacy, SCCs, BCRs, consent, certification). Where no adequacy exists, alternative mechanisms are in place before transfer commences. Standard contractual clauses or other contractual safeguards are executed before initiating transfers. DPO approval is obtained for each mechanism selection with documented rationale. Transfer mechanisms are registered with the Commissioner where required.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Transferring personal data without a valid transfer mechanism violates s129. Commissioner may order the transfer to cease. Contractual gaps with foreign recipients leave personal data unprotected. Penalty exposure up to RM300,000 and/or 2 years imprisonment under s5(2).
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the cross-border transfer register. For 10 transfers, verify the selected transfer mechanism is documented and appropriate. For adequacy-based transfers, confirm the recipient country is on the Commissioner's approved list. For SCC-based transfers, obtain copies of executed SCCs. Verify DPO approval documentation for 5 mechanism selections.

controls.name: Transfer Impact Assessment (TIA)
controls.description: Conduct Transfer Impact Assessments for transfers to third countries without adequacy determinations to assess whether the chosen transfer mechanism can effectively protect personal data in the destination country's legal environment, particularly assessing government access to data
keyActivities: Assess the destination country's data protection law, government access powers, and enforcement environment
Evaluate whether the chosen transfer mechanism (SCCs, BCRs) can be upheld in the destination country
Identify supplementary measures (encryption, pseudonymisation, contractual enhancements) where TIA reveals gaps
Document TIA findings, supplementary measures adopted, and residual risk acceptance
Review TIA when significant changes occur in the destination country's legal environment

maturity.basic: Basic research of destination country legal environment by DPO or legal; documented in transfer register; supplementary measures identified ad hoc
maturity.mature: Structured TIA template covering legal framework, government access, enforcement, and supplementary measures; DPO and legal sign-off on TIA; TIA stored in transfer register; review trigger for material legal changes in destination country
maturity.advanced: TIA integrated into cross-border transfer workflow with country risk intelligence feeds; automated alerts on significant legal changes in transfer destinations; TIA library covering common destination countries; third-party legal opinions obtained for high-risk transfers; TIA outcomes inform encryption and access control requirements

toolExamples: DLA Piper Global Data Protection Law
Custom TIA template with country research

aviation.additionalRequirements: PNR/API transfers to foreign authorities require documentation under s129; adequacy or derogation basis required

healthcare.additionalRequirements: Overseas telehealth providers must have s129-compliant transfer mechanisms

controls.controlObjective: Assess whether the chosen transfer mechanism can effectively protect personal data in the destination country's legal environment for transfers to countries without adequacy determinations, particularly evaluating government access risks to personal data.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: A Transfer Impact Assessment is conducted for each transfer to a non-adequate country. The TIA assesses the destination country's data protection law, government access powers, and enforcement environment. Supplementary measures (encryption, pseudonymisation, contractual enhancements) are identified where the TIA reveals gaps. TIA findings, supplementary measures, and residual risk acceptance are documented. TIAs are reviewed when significant changes occur in the destination country's legal environment.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Transfer mechanisms may be ineffective in the destination country's legal environment. Government access to personal data may undermine contractual protections. Without TIA, the organization cannot demonstrate it assessed transfer risk. Commissioner may view untested transfers as reckless.
controls.samplingGuidance: Identify all transfers to non-adequate countries from the transfer register. Sample 5 and verify a TIA was conducted. Review TIA content for assessment of local law, government access, and supplementary measures. Verify TIA review triggers are defined. For 2 TIAs, assess whether identified supplementary measures were implemented.

controls.name: Ongoing Cross-Border Transfer Monitoring
controls.description: Monitor existing cross-border transfer arrangements on an ongoing basis to ensure continued adequacy of transfer mechanisms, detect changes in the legal environment of destination countries, and identify new or unauthorized transfers requiring assessment and authorization
keyActivities: Review cross-border transfer register quarterly for accuracy and completeness
Monitor Commissioner announcements and regulatory updates affecting approved country list
Monitor legal developments in key transfer destination countries (legislation, court decisions)
Conduct annual re-assessment of transfer mechanisms for significant cross-border transfers
Implement process for employees to declare new cross-border data flows before implementation

maturity.basic: Annual review of transfer register by DPO; Commissioner website checked for approved country list updates; new vendors required to disclose international transfers to DPO
maturity.mature: Quarterly transfer register review with documented outcomes; regulatory monitoring service subscription covering key jurisdictions; annual mechanism re-assessment for top-10 cross-border transfers; IT procurement gate requires DPO approval for new international data flows
maturity.advanced: Real-time regulatory monitoring with automated alerts for transfer-relevant developments; CASB monitoring for unauthorized international data flows; automated annual re-assessment workflow; cross-border transfer risk score in third-party risk management dashboard; management reporting on cross-border transfer risk

aviation.additionalRequirements: PNR/API transfers to foreign authorities require documentation under s129; adequacy or derogation basis required

healthcare.additionalRequirements: Overseas telehealth providers must have s129-compliant transfer mechanisms

controls.controlObjective: Monitor existing cross-border transfer arrangements on an ongoing basis to ensure continued adequacy of transfer mechanisms, detect changes in destination country legal environments, and identify new or unauthorized transfers requiring assessment under s129.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: The cross-border transfer register is reviewed quarterly for accuracy and completeness. Commissioner announcements and regulatory updates affecting the approved country list are monitored. Legal developments in key transfer destination countries are tracked. Annual re-assessment is conducted for significant cross-border transfers. A process exists for employees to declare new cross-border data flows before implementation.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Transfer mechanisms may become inadequate due to legal changes in destination countries. New unauthorized transfers may commence without assessment. Approved country list changes may not be detected, leaving transfers operating on an invalid basis.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review evidence of quarterly transfer register reviews for the last 4 quarters. Verify the monitoring process for Commissioner announcements and adequacy list changes. Sample 3 significant transfers and verify annual re-assessment was conducted. Check for any new vendors onboarded in the last 6 months and verify cross-border transfer assessment was completed.

controls.name: Data Processor Due Diligence
controls.description: Conduct privacy and security due diligence on prospective data processors before engagement, assessing their technical and organizational measures to protect personal data and their ability to support the organization's PDPA compliance obligations
keyActivities: Classify all third parties by data access level and processing sensitivity
Send privacy due diligence questionnaire (covering security, privacy practices, subprocessors, cross-border transfers) to all significant processors
Review security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, CSA STAR) as part of assessment
Assess processor's ability to support data subject rights requests and breach notification
Document assessment findings and risk rating before contract execution

maturity.basic: Due diligence questionnaire sent to processors handling significant personal data; certification copies reviewed; DPO approves engagement based on questionnaire responses
maturity.mature: Tiered due diligence programme based on processor risk rating; standardised questionnaire with scoring rubric; certification review against recognized standards; annual reassessment for high-risk processors; findings documented in third-party register
maturity.advanced: Automated due diligence platform with continuous processor risk scoring; integration with third-party risk intelligence services (BitSight, SecurityScorecard); onsite audit capability for critical processors; SLA for questionnaire completion; AI-assisted analysis of processor privacy policies and certifications

controls.controlObjective: Assess prospective data processors' technical and organizational measures before engagement, ensuring they can protect personal data and support the organization's PDPA compliance obligations under s9.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: All third parties are classified by data access level and processing sensitivity. A privacy due diligence questionnaire covering security, privacy practices, subprocessors, and cross-border transfers is sent to all significant processors. Security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II) are reviewed. The processor's ability to support DSARs and breach notification is assessed. Assessment findings and risk rating are documented before contract execution.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Engaging a processor without due diligence exposes personal data to unknown security risks. The data user remains liable under PDPA for processor failures. A breach at an unvetted processor reflects poorly on the organization's s9 compliance. Commissioner may view absence of due diligence as negligence.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the processor register. Sample 10 processors and verify due diligence was completed before engagement. For each, verify questionnaire completion, certification review, and documented risk rating. Review 3 high-risk processor assessments in detail. Verify the due diligence process is integrated with vendor onboarding.

controls.name: Data Processing Agreement Lifecycle Management
controls.description: Ensure all data processors are subject to a binding data processing agreement (DPA) containing the mandatory provisions required to ensure the processor processes personal data only on the data controller's instructions and implements adequate security measures
keyActivities: Maintain a DPA template incorporating all minimum required provisions
Require DPA execution before any personal data is processed by a third-party processor
Review and update DPA template annually or following material regulatory changes
Track DPA execution status and renewal dates in the third-party processor register
Ensure DPAs with subprocessors of processors are in place and meet equivalent standards

maturity.basic: Standard DPA template reviewed by legal; DPAs executed for all identified processors; contracts stored in shared drive; manual tracking of DPA status
maturity.mature: DPA template library for different processing scenarios; automated contract expiry alerts; DPA clause library enabling negotiation while maintaining minimum standards; DPA status tracked in procurement system; annual DPA template review against regulatory guidance
maturity.advanced: AI-assisted contract review to identify DPA gaps in existing vendor agreements; automated DPA workflow integrated with vendor onboarding; contractual obligation tracking (breach notification SLAs, audit rights) in contract management platform; DPA compliance dashboard

controls.controlObjective: Ensure all data processors are bound by a data processing agreement containing mandatory provisions for processing instructions, security measures, subprocessor controls, breach notification, and audit rights, protecting personal data throughout the processing chain.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: A DPA template incorporating all minimum required provisions is maintained. DPA execution is required before any personal data is processed by a third party. The DPA template is reviewed annually or following material regulatory changes. DPA execution status and renewal dates are tracked in the processor register. DPAs with subprocessors meet equivalent standards.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Without a DPA, the data user has no contractual basis to enforce security standards or processing instructions on the processor. Breach notification obligations may not flow through to the processor. The organization cannot demonstrate it took reasonable steps under s9 to secure data processed by third parties.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the processor register. For 15 processors, verify a DPA is executed and on file. Review 5 DPAs for mandatory clause completeness (processing instructions, security, subprocessors, breach notification, audit rights, data return/deletion). Verify the DPA template was reviewed in the last 12 months. Check for any processors processing data without a DPA.

controls.name: Ongoing Processor Oversight and Audit
controls.description: Maintain ongoing oversight of data processors through annual reassessments, review of updated certifications, and exercise of contractual audit rights where risk warrants, ensuring processors continue to meet the organization's data protection requirements
keyActivities: Conduct annual reassessment of high-risk processors using updated security questionnaire
Review processor security certifications for continued validity and scope coverage
Exercise contractual audit rights for critical processors handling large volumes of sensitive personal data
Review processor incident and breach reports and assess impact on the organization
Escalate processor compliance issues and document remediation requirements

maturity.basic: Annual email to key processors requesting updated security questionnaire; ISO 27001 certificate copies collected; issues escalated to DPO
maturity.mature: Tiered reassessment schedule (annual for high-risk, bi-annual for medium-risk); certification scope verification; contractual audit right exercised for top-5 critical processors annually; processor compliance dashboard in third-party register
maturity.advanced: Continuous security rating monitoring via BitSight/SecurityScorecard; automated reassessment workflow triggered by certification expiry or security rating decline; onsite or virtual audit program for critical processors; processor risk metrics integrated into organizational risk register; board reporting on third-party data risk

toolExamples: Contract management with audit right tracking
controls.controlObjective: Maintain ongoing oversight of data processors through annual reassessments, certification reviews, and exercise of contractual audit rights, ensuring processors continue to meet data protection requirements under s9.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Annual reassessment of high-risk processors uses an updated security questionnaire. Processor security certifications are reviewed for continued validity and scope coverage. Contractual audit rights are exercised for critical processors handling large volumes of sensitive data. Processor incident and breach reports are reviewed for organizational impact. Processor compliance issues are escalated with documented remediation requirements.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Processor security posture may deteriorate after initial onboarding. Expired certifications may indicate lapsed security controls. Without ongoing oversight, the organization is blind to processor compliance drift. A breach at an unsupervised processor exposes the organization to enforcement action under s9.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the processor oversight schedule. Sample 10 processors and verify annual reassessment was completed for the current year. For 5 processors, verify certification validity and scope. Review evidence of contractual audit right exercise for the top 3 critical processors. Review 3 processor incident reports and organizational impact assessments.

controls.name: Data Processor Offboarding and Data Return
controls.description: Implement a structured offboarding process for outgoing data processors ensuring all personal data held by the processor is returned or securely deleted, access credentials are revoked, and certificate of deletion is obtained in compliance with DPA terms and PDPA s10
keyActivities: Initiate offboarding checklist at contract termination or notice date
Request data return or deletion per DPA provisions (return first, then confirm deletion)
Obtain written certificate of data deletion from outgoing processor
Revoke all system access and API credentials on effective termination date
Archive DPA, due diligence records, and offboarding documentation in third-party register

maturity.basic: DPO emails outgoing processor to confirm data deletion; certificate received and filed; IT revokes access credentials
maturity.mature: Processor offboarding checklist with sign-off required before contract close; deletion certificate template in DPA; IT access revocation tracked; 30-day follow-up if certificate not received
maturity.advanced: Automated offboarding workflow integrated with contract management; technical verification of data deletion via API or audit log review where available; deletion certificate stored in third-party register; post-offboarding security scan for residual data; management reporting on offboarding completion rates

toolExamples: Contract lifecycle management with offboarding workflow
controls.controlObjective: Ensure all personal data held by outgoing processors is returned or securely deleted upon contract termination, with access revoked and deletion certified, in compliance with DPA terms and s10 Retention Principle.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: An offboarding checklist is initiated at contract termination. Data return or deletion is requested per DPA provisions. A written certificate of data deletion is obtained from the outgoing processor. All system access and API credentials are revoked on the effective termination date. DPA, due diligence records, and offboarding documentation are archived in the processor register.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Personal data remains with former processors without contractual controls or security obligations. Data may be retained indefinitely or misused. Access credentials may remain active, creating unauthorized access risk. Without deletion certificates, the organization cannot demonstrate compliance with s10.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review processor offboarding records for the last 12 months. For all offboarded processors, verify the offboarding checklist was completed, deletion certificate obtained, and access credentials revoked. For 3 cases, verify data return or deletion was confirmed. Check for any processors terminated more than 30 days ago without completed offboarding.

controls.name: Data Protection Officer Appointment and Functions
controls.description: Appoint a qualified Data Protection Officer (DPO) as required under PDPA s12A (inserted by Amendment Act A1727), register the DPO with the Commissioner, and ensure the DPO performs all required functions including advising on compliance, monitoring implementation, and acting as contact point for data subjects and the Commissioner
keyActivities: Assess mandatory DPO appointment obligation under s12A criteria and DPO Guideline 2025
Select and appoint DPO meeting minimum qualification requirements
Register appointed DPO with the Commissioner within prescribed period
Provide DPO with sufficient resources, authority, and independence to perform functions
Ensure DPO performs annual compliance monitoring and reports to senior management

maturity.basic: DPO appointed and registered; DPO performs compliance functions part-time alongside another role; annual report to management; Commissioner contact point established
maturity.mature: Dedicated DPO role with clear job description and reporting line to board; DPO with recognized certification (CIPM, CIPP); DPO participates in DPIA reviews, policy updates, incident response; DPO annual report with metrics presented to board
maturity.advanced: DPO function with supporting privacy team; DPO integrated into all major business decisions involving personal data; DPO maintains professional development plan; DPO participates in industry associations and regulatory engagement; DPO dashboard with real-time compliance metrics

toolExamples: GRC platforms for DPO function management
controls.controlObjective: Appoint a qualified DPO as required under s12A (inserted by A1727), register the DPO with the Commissioner, and ensure the DPO performs all statutory functions including compliance monitoring, advice, and acting as contact point for data subjects and the Commissioner.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: DPO appointment obligation is assessed against s12A criteria and DPO Guideline 2025. A qualified DPO is appointed and registered with the Commissioner within the prescribed period. The DPO has sufficient resources, authority, and independence. The DPO performs annual compliance monitoring and reports to senior management. DPO contact information is published in all privacy notices and on the organization's website.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Failure to appoint a mandatory DPO violates s12A. Commissioner may issue enforcement notice. The organization lacks a designated compliance function for data protection. Without DPO registration, the Commissioner cannot communicate regulatory requirements. Penalty exposure under s12A provisions.
controls.samplingGuidance: Verify DPO appointment documentation and registration with Commissioner. Review DPO qualifications against s12A requirements. Verify DPO has a documented job description, reporting line, and resource allocation. Review the DPO's most recent annual compliance report. Verify DPO contact details are published in privacy notices and on the website.

controls.name: Privacy Policy and Procedure Management
controls.description: Maintain a suite of up-to-date data protection policies and procedures covering all PDPA obligations, ensuring policies are approved at appropriate levels, communicated to staff, regularly reviewed, and aligned with the current regulatory environment including the 2024 Amendment Act A1727
keyActivities: Maintain a policy inventory covering all data protection obligations under PDPA
Conduct annual review of all policies against current PDPA requirements and Commissioner guidance
Obtain board or senior management approval for the overarching Data Protection Policy
Communicate policy updates to affected staff within a reasonable period of approval
Retire superseded policies and maintain archive of prior versions with effective dates

maturity.basic: Core data protection policy exists; updated when significant regulatory changes occur; stored on intranet; staff notified by email of major changes
maturity.mature: Complete policy suite covering all PDPA domains; annual policy review cycle with DPO sign-off; board approval for key policies; policy acknowledgement tracked in HR system; policy version control in document management system
maturity.advanced: Policy management platform with automated review reminders and approval workflows; regulatory change monitoring service triggers policy review; policy gap analysis against PDPA amendments; real-time policy compliance indicators; external counsel review of policy suite on significant regulatory change

controls.controlObjective: Maintain a comprehensive suite of data protection policies covering all PDPA obligations, approved at appropriate levels, communicated to staff, and regularly reviewed to reflect the current regulatory environment including Amendment A1727.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: A policy inventory covers all data protection obligations under PDPA. Annual review of all policies against current requirements and Commissioner guidance is conducted with DPO sign-off. The overarching Data Protection Policy has board or senior management approval. Policy updates are communicated to affected staff within a reasonable period. Superseded policies are retired and archived with effective dates.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Outdated or incomplete policies leave staff without guidance on current obligations. New A1727 requirements (breach notification, DPO, data portability) may not be operationalised without policy updates. Commissioner may view absence of policies as evidence of governance failure under s5.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the policy inventory for completeness against PDPA domains. Sample 5 policies and verify annual review with DPO sign-off in the last 12 months. Verify board approval for the overarching Data Protection Policy. Check policy communication records for the most recent update. Verify superseded policy archive with version history.

controls.name: Staff Training and Awareness Programme
controls.description: Implement a structured data protection training and awareness programme ensuring all employees understand their PDPA obligations, can recognize personal data and sensitive personal data, know how to handle data subject requests, and can identify and report potential breaches
keyActivities: Deliver mandatory data protection induction training to all new employees within 30 days of start
Deliver annual refresher training to all staff covering key PDPA obligations and organizational policies
Develop role-specific training modules for DPO, IT security, HR, marketing, and customer service
Track training completions in training register and follow up on non-completions
Assess training effectiveness through knowledge assessments and breach/DSAR performance metrics

maturity.basic: Annual mandatory e-learning module on data protection basics; completion tracked by HR; new employee induction covers PDPA overview
maturity.mature: Role-differentiated training curriculum; compliance-themed scenarios in training content; assessment with minimum pass mark; non-completion escalated to line manager; training records integrated with HR system; phishing simulation to reinforce security awareness
maturity.advanced: Adaptive learning platform with AI-personalised training content by role and past assessment performance; micro-learning nudges on current breach scenarios; gamified compliance challenges; real-time training dashboard; training effectiveness measured against incident and DSAR performance trends; external certification support for privacy professionals

controls.controlObjective: Ensure all employees understand their PDPA obligations through structured training covering personal data identification, data subject request handling, and breach reporting, reducing human error as a breach vector under s9.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Mandatory data protection induction training is delivered to all new employees within 30 days. Annual refresher training covers key PDPA obligations and organizational policies. Role-specific training modules exist for DPO, IT security, HR, marketing, and customer service. Training completions are tracked and non-completions followed up. Training effectiveness is assessed through knowledge tests and linked to breach/DSAR performance metrics.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Untrained staff are the primary source of human-error breaches (misdirected emails, improper disclosure, lost devices). Staff who cannot recognize DSARs cause missed deadlines. Commissioner may view absence of training as failure to implement adequate organizational measures under s9.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review training completion rates for the last 12 months. Verify 90%+ completion rate for mandatory training. Sample 10 new employees and verify induction training was completed within 30 days. Review role-specific training content for 2 high-risk roles (HR, customer service). Review knowledge assessment pass rates and remediation for failures.

controls.name: Privacy by Design and Default
controls.description: Embed privacy considerations into the design and development of new products, services, and systems from inception, ensuring data protection requirements are addressed by design rather than as an afterthought, aligned with the accountability principles underlying PDPA compliance
keyActivities: Define privacy by design trigger criteria for new projects and material changes to existing systems
Conduct privacy design review using structured checklist at the design/architecture stage
Perform DPIA for high-risk processing activities identified at design stage
Obtain DPO sign-off as a mandatory gate before go-live for in-scope projects
Document privacy design decisions and accepted residual risks for each project

maturity.basic: DPO consulted on major new IT projects; privacy considerations checked against PDPA principles before launch; ad hoc documentation
maturity.mature: Privacy by design checklist integrated into project governance; DPIA trigger assessment mandatory for all new projects processing personal data; DPO review required for significant processing changes; privacy by design documented in project artefacts
maturity.advanced: Privacy engineering function embedded in product and IT teams; automated privacy gate in CI/CD pipeline; privacy design patterns library; DPIA integrated with project management tooling; privacy debt tracking for legacy systems; privacy metrics in product health dashboards

toolExamples: Threat modelling tools (Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool)
controls.controlObjective: Embed privacy considerations into the design of new products, services, and systems from inception, ensuring data protection requirements are addressed by design rather than as an afterthought, aligned with PDPA accountability principles under s5, s6, and s9.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Privacy by design trigger criteria are defined for new projects and material changes. A privacy design review using a structured checklist is conducted at the design/architecture stage. DPIA is performed for high-risk processing activities identified at design stage. DPO sign-off is a mandatory gate before go-live. Privacy design decisions and accepted residual risks are documented.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Privacy requirements bolted on after development are costly to implement and often incomplete. Systems launched without privacy review may violate PDPA from day one. DPIAs not conducted for high-risk processing miss the opportunity to mitigate risk before harm occurs. Commissioner may view absence of privacy by design as governance failure.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the project register for the last 12 months. Identify all projects that involved personal data processing. For 5 projects, verify a privacy design review was conducted, DPIA completed where triggered, and DPO sign-off obtained before go-live. Review 2 DPIA reports for quality and completeness.

controls.name: Compliance Monitoring and Internal Audit
controls.description: Implement a compliance monitoring programme and annual internal audit of data protection practices to identify gaps against PDPA obligations, assess control effectiveness, and provide management with accurate information on the organization's compliance posture
keyActivities: Develop and execute an annual data protection audit programme based on risk priorities
Assess compliance against PDPA obligations using a structured compliance checklist
Review sample of consent records, DSARs, breach records, and training completions
Rate audit findings by severity and agree remediation timelines with management
Report audit findings and management responses to board or audit committee

maturity.basic: Annual self-assessment by DPO against PDPA checklist; findings reported to management; ad hoc remediation plans
maturity.mature: Annual compliance audit using structured methodology; internal audit or DPO-led; evidence-based assessment with sampling; findings rated by severity; remediation tracking to closure; board reporting
maturity.advanced: Risk-based audit programme with continuous monitoring between annual audits; automated compliance testing (consent record completeness, DSAR response rates, training completion); external verification for high-risk areas; audit outcomes feed into DPO annual report; benchmarking against industry peers and Commissioner guidance

controls.controlObjective: Identify compliance gaps and assess control effectiveness through a structured compliance monitoring programme and annual internal audit of data protection practices, providing management with accurate information on PDPA compliance posture.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: An annual data protection audit programme is developed based on risk priorities. Compliance is assessed against PDPA obligations using a structured checklist. Samples of consent records, DSARs, breach records, and training completions are reviewed. Findings are rated by severity with agreed remediation timelines. Audit findings and management responses are reported to the board or audit committee.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Without monitoring, compliance gaps go undetected until a breach or complaint triggers investigation. Management lacks accurate information for decision-making on data protection investment. Commissioner may view absence of self-monitoring as evidence of negligence. Audit findings from Commissioner inspection will be more severe.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the annual audit programme and verify it was executed. Review the most recent audit report. Verify findings are rated by severity and remediation timelines agreed. Track 5 audit findings to closure. Review board/audit committee reporting on data protection audit results. Verify the audit scope covers all PDPA domains.

controls.name: Board and Senior Management Reporting and Accountability
controls.description: Establish regular reporting of data protection compliance status, key risks, incidents, and emerging obligations to senior management and the board, ensuring leadership accountability for PDPA compliance and informed decision-making on data protection investment
keyActivities: Prepare and present DPO annual report to board covering compliance status, incidents, and recommendations
Provide quarterly management reporting on key data protection metrics (breach incidents, DSAR performance, training rates)
Ensure material data breaches are reported to board within 24 hours of notification decision
Include data protection risk in the organization's risk register with board visibility
Escalate regulatory changes and enforcement actions to board with assessment of impact

maturity.basic: Annual DPO report presented to management; significant incidents escalated ad hoc; data protection risk in IT risk register
maturity.mature: Quarterly management KPI dashboard for data protection; DPO annual report tabled at board; breach incidents reported to board within required timeframes; data protection risk in enterprise risk register with board visibility; management sign-off on DPO recommendations
maturity.advanced: Real-time executive data protection dashboard; monthly management briefing on emerging risks; board education sessions on PDPA and regulatory developments; data protection risk appetite statement approved by board; DPO participates in board audit/risk committee meetings; external benchmarking of compliance maturity presented to board

toolExamples: GRC platforms with board reporting module
PowerBI / Tableau for data protection dashboards
controls.controlObjective: Provide senior management and the board with regular reporting on data protection compliance status, risks, incidents, and emerging obligations, ensuring leadership accountability under s5 and informed decision-making on data protection investment.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: The DPO annual report is presented to the board covering compliance status, incidents, and recommendations. Quarterly management reporting covers key metrics (breach incidents, DSAR performance, training rates). Material data breaches are reported to the board within 24 hours of the notification decision. Data protection risk is included in the organizational risk register with board visibility. Regulatory changes and enforcement actions are escalated to the board with impact assessment.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Leadership is uninformed about compliance status and exposure. Data protection is not factored into strategic decisions. Board members face personal liability risk under s130-s131 (officer liability for corporate offences). Absence of board reporting suggests data protection is not treated as an organizational priority.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the DPO annual report for the last 2 years. Verify quarterly management reporting with key metrics. Check board meeting minutes for data protection agenda items. Verify material breach reporting to board within 24 hours. Review the organizational risk register for data protection risk entries with board visibility.

controls.name: Data Controller Registration Management
controls.description: Manage the organization's data controller registration with the Personal Data Protection Commissioner under PDPA s13-s16, including initial registration, timely renewal, notification of material changes, and DPO registration, to ensure the organization operates with valid registration at all times
keyActivities: Determine registration obligation and class under the Personal Data Protection (Class of Data Controllers) Order 2013
Apply for registration before commencing processing activities as a registered class data controller
Monitor registration expiry date and submit renewal application before expiry
Notify Commissioner of material changes to registered particulars within prescribed period
Register DPO with Commissioner as required under s12A

maturity.basic: DPO tracks registration renewal date manually; renewal submitted annually; certificate stored in compliance files; material changes notified when identified
maturity.mature: Registration calendar with automated renewal alerts at 90 and 30 days before expiry; registration details reviewed annually against ROPA for accuracy; material change notification checklist; DPO registration maintained current
maturity.advanced: Registration compliance integrated into GRC platform with automated tracking; change management process flags registration notification obligations; Commissioner portal monitored for regulatory updates to registration requirements; registration compliance included in annual audit scope

toolExamples: GRC platform for regulatory deadline tracking
controls.controlObjective: Maintain valid registration with the Commissioner under s13-s16, including initial registration, timely renewal, notification of material changes, and DPO registration, ensuring the organization operates lawfully at all times.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Registration obligation and class are determined under the Class of Data Controllers Order 2013. Registration is obtained before commencing processing. Registration expiry is monitored and renewal submitted before expiry. Material changes to registered particulars are notified to the Commissioner within the prescribed period. DPO registration is maintained current under s12A.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Operating without valid registration is a criminal offence under s5(1) of the PDPA. Penalty of fine up to RM500,000 and/or imprisonment up to 3 years. Lapsed registration creates a period of unlawful processing. Commissioner may publicize non-registration.
controls.samplingGuidance: Verify current registration certificate and expiry date. Confirm renewal was submitted before the last expiry. Verify registered particulars match current organizational details. Confirm DPO registration is current. Review process for monitoring registration expiry and material change notification.

controls.name: Regulatory Inspection Readiness
controls.description: Maintain the organization in a state of readiness for inspections, investigations, and audits by the JPDP Commissioner under PDPA Part VIII (s101-s109), including pre-organized compliance evidence, trained inspection response team, and documented response procedures
keyActivities: Maintain a compliance evidence repository with current copies of all key policies, registers, and records
Designate and train an inspection response team including DPO, legal counsel, and IT representative
Conduct annual mock inspection exercise to test readiness and identify gaps
Document inspection response procedures including immediate notification, document preservation, and liaison protocols
Review and update inspection readiness plan following each regulatory engagement or industry enforcement action

maturity.basic: DPO coordinates inspection response; key documents accessible; legal counsel on retainer; response guided by DPO judgment
maturity.mature: Written inspection response procedure; designated inspection response team with defined roles; compliance evidence repository maintained and indexed; annual readiness review; external legal counsel briefed on PDPA enforcement procedures
maturity.advanced: Formal inspection readiness programme with annual tabletop simulation; comprehensive compliance evidence repository with document index; privilege review procedure for legal communications; inspection response playbook covering all JPDP inspection powers; post-inspection review and learning embedded in governance programme

toolExamples: Document management system for evidence repository
controls.controlObjective: Maintain the organization in a state of readiness for JPDP Commissioner inspections and investigations under Part VIII (s101-s109), with pre-organized evidence, a trained response team, and documented procedures to ensure cooperative and orderly engagement.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: A compliance evidence repository contains current copies of all key policies, registers, and records. An inspection response team is designated and trained, including DPO, legal counsel, and IT representative. Annual mock inspection exercises test readiness and identify gaps. Inspection response procedures document immediate notification, document preservation, and liaison protocols. The readiness plan is updated following each regulatory engagement.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Disorganized response to Commissioner inspection creates a negative impression and extends the inspection duration. Key evidence may not be locatable under time pressure. Staff may inadvertently disclose privileged information. Obstruction of an inspection is a criminal offence under s105.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the compliance evidence repository for currency and completeness. Verify the inspection response team is designated with defined roles. Check for evidence of annual mock inspection exercise. Review the inspection response procedure document. Verify the last regulatory engagement (if any) resulted in an updated readiness plan.

controls.name: Enforcement Notice Compliance Management
controls.description: Manage compliance with enforcement notices issued by the Commissioner under PDPA Part IX, including legal assessment, compliance roadmap development, evidence collection, and Commissioner reporting, to avoid the criminal offence of non-compliance under PDPA s133
keyActivities: Immediately escalate enforcement notices to DPO, senior management, and legal counsel upon receipt
Obtain legal advice on notice requirements, compliance obligations, and appeal grounds
Develop a costed compliance roadmap with milestones within the notice compliance period
Maintain regular communication with Commissioner on compliance progress where appropriate
Compile and submit compliance evidence to Commissioner by the required deadline

maturity.basic: DPO and legal review enforcement notice; compliance actions assigned; evidence compiled and submitted to Commissioner by deadline
maturity.mature: Enforcement notice response procedure documented and tested; compliance roadmap template with milestone tracking; legal sign-off on Commissioner communications; post-compliance review to prevent recurrence
maturity.advanced: Enforcement notice risk assessment integrated with proactive compliance programme to prevent notices; rapid legal response protocol (within 24 hours); compliance roadmap managed via project management tool; Commissioner relationship management strategy; enforcement notice outcome used to update audit programme and training content

toolExamples: Project management tools (MS Project, Jira) for compliance roadmap
controls.controlObjective: Manage compliance with enforcement notices issued by the Commissioner under Part IX (s110-s111), developing and executing a compliance roadmap within the notice period, to avoid the criminal offence of non-compliance under s133.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Enforcement notices are immediately escalated to DPO, senior management, and legal counsel. Legal advice is obtained on notice requirements, compliance obligations, and appeal grounds. A costed compliance roadmap with milestones is developed within the notice compliance period. Regular communication with the Commissioner on compliance progress is maintained. Compliance evidence is compiled and submitted by the required deadline.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Non-compliance with an enforcement notice is a criminal offence under s133, carrying a fine up to RM500,000 and/or imprisonment up to 3 years. Commissioner may pursue prosecution. Non-compliance may also trigger registration revocation. Public disclosure of enforcement action causes severe reputational damage.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review any enforcement notices received in the last 3 years. If received, verify legal review was conducted, compliance roadmap developed, milestones tracked, Commissioner communications maintained, and evidence submitted by deadline. If no notices received, review the enforcement notice response procedure for completeness and test via tabletop exercise.

controls.name: Regulatory Monitoring and Change Management
controls.description: Systematically monitor PDPA regulatory developments including Commissioner guidelines, codes of practice, new subsidiary legislation, enforcement actions, and relevant court decisions, and assess their impact on the organization's compliance programme with timely implementation of required changes
keyActivities: Subscribe to and review JPDP Commissioner publications, press releases, and enforcement announcements
Monitor Parliament Hansard and Gazette for PDPA amendments and subsidiary legislation
Assess impact of each regulatory development on organizational policies, procedures, and controls
Implement required compliance changes within appropriate timeframes
Brief management and board on material regulatory developments and compliance implications

maturity.basic: DPO subscribes to JPDP email alerts; external legal counsel engaged for major regulatory changes; updates incorporated into policies annually
maturity.mature: Formal regulatory monitoring programme covering JPDP, Parliament, and comparable regional regulators; impact assessment for each significant development; change management procedure for implementing required updates; management briefings on significant changes
maturity.advanced: Regulatory technology platform monitoring Malaysian, ASEAN, and global privacy regulatory developments with AI-assisted impact analysis; regulatory change management integrated with policy management platform; regulatory intelligence briefings to board; engagement in Commissioner consultation processes; participation in industry associations for advance regulatory intelligence

toolExamples: Data Protection World Forum news alerts
controls.controlObjective: Systematically monitor PDPA regulatory developments — Commissioner guidelines, codes of practice, subsidiary legislation, enforcement actions, and court decisions — to assess impact on the compliance programme and implement required changes proactively.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: JPDP Commissioner publications, press releases, and enforcement announcements are reviewed regularly. Parliament Hansard and Gazette are monitored for PDPA amendments and subsidiary legislation. Impact of each regulatory development on organizational policies, procedures, and controls is assessed. Required compliance changes are implemented within appropriate timeframes. Management and board are briefed on material regulatory developments.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Regulatory changes may go undetected, leaving the organization non-compliant. New Commissioner guidelines may impose additional requirements that are not implemented. Enforcement actions against other organizations may signal Commissioner priorities that the organization fails to address. Management is uninformed about the evolving regulatory landscape.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the regulatory monitoring log for the last 12 months. Verify key regulatory developments were captured (Commissioner guidelines, A1727 implementation guidance, enforcement actions). Sample 3 regulatory developments and verify impact assessment was conducted and any required changes implemented. Review management briefing records on material developments.

controls.name: DPbD Lifecycle Integration
controls.description: Embed data protection considerations across the full processing lifecycle — design, development, deployment, operation, and decommissioning — so privacy is built in rather than retrofitted. Aligned with the JPDP DPbD Guideline (issued 30 April 2026) and its 4 elements (Proactiveness, End-to-end Protection, Transparency, User-centricity) per Section 4. Note: Cavoukian's 7 foundational principles are NOT adopted by the issued guideline.
keyActivities: Adopt a DPbD policy/standard signed off by senior management
Embed a DPbD checklist in the SDLC requirements gate (and in procurement for vendor-developed solutions)
Conduct DPIA where the processing meets the quantitative or qualitative thresholds (cross-link with dpia/)
Build privacy controls (encryption, pseudonymisation, access control, audit logging, rights-handling) into the system from day one
Operate go-live gate with DPO/security/legal sign-off before deployment
Maintain RoPA reflecting the lifecycle stage of each processing activity
Run periodic DPbD validation reviews on operational systems
Apply secure decommissioning with certificates of destruction and third-party deletion attestations

maturity.basic: DPbD considered ad-hoc by IT/legal at major project milestones; no formal checklist; DPIA conducted reactively when external pressure surfaces
maturity.mature: DPbD policy in place; checklist embedded at SDLC requirements and go-live gates; DPIAs triggered systematically; DPO sign-off recorded; lifecycle stage tracked in RoPA
maturity.advanced: DPbD integrated into automated governance (policy-as-code in CI/CD); pre-commit privacy linting; automated DPIA scoring; central DPbD compliance dashboard; vendor onboarding gated on DPbD attestation; lifecycle-aware retention triggers and decommissioning automation

toolExamples: Custom DPbD checklist embedded in JIRA/Azure DevOps
controls.controlObjective: Ensure data protection is integrated into every stage of every processing activity's lifecycle — from initial design through to decommissioning — so privacy outcomes are built in by default, evidenced, and auditable.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: DPbD policy approved at senior management, referencing the 4 elements per Section 4 of the issued Guideline. DPbD checklist embedded in SDLC requirements gate and go-live gate. DPIA triggered systematically per dpia/thresholds.json (or for any ADMP regardless of subject count per ADMP §6.1). DPO sign-off recorded for high-risk projects. Lifecycle stage tracked in RoPA. Decommissioning followed with certificates of destruction and backup purge attestations. Periodic DPbD validation reviews of operational systems with documented findings and remediation. Section 13 governance practices in place: board expertise/training, senior accountability assignment, DPO meeting cadence, board reporting, performance evaluation KPIs.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Privacy controls retrofitted (or absent), creating gaps that materialise as data breaches, unlawful processing, excessive collection, indefinite retention, and inability to honour data subject rights. DPIA either skipped or conducted superficially. Decommissioning leaves orphan data in backups and third-party systems. Senior management cannot demonstrate accountability under s5.
controls.samplingGuidance: Sample 5 projects launched in the last 12 months. For each, verify (1) DPbD checklist completed at requirements gate, (2) DPIA conducted where threshold met, (3) DPO sign-off prior to go-live, (4) RoPA entry created and maintained, (5) operational privacy monitoring in place. Review the most recent DPbD policy review. Sample 2 decommissioned systems and verify destruction certificates and backup purge.

controls.name: Privacy as Default and Data Minimisation
controls.description: Configure all processing systems so that the most privacy-protective settings apply by default and only the minimum personal data necessary for each specified purpose is collected, used, retained, and disclosed. Operationalises Element 1.4.2.2 of the issued Guideline (privacy by default within the Proactiveness element) and §5.4(c) data minimisation under the General Principle (s6).
keyActivities: Default to no collection unless a specific compelling purpose is defined and documented
Implement opt-in (not opt-out) consent for non-essential processing
Pre-tick only strictly-necessary cookies; secondary cookies require affirmative consent
Set the most privacy-protective defaults across system configuration (visibility, sharing, profile)
Differentiate mandatory vs optional fields on collection forms and minimise mandatory fields
Apply purpose limitation enforcement: separate databases or logical isolation between purposes
Apply pseudonymisation as soon as direct identification is no longer needed
Periodic review of data fields against necessity for current purposes; delete redundant fields

maturity.basic: Forms collect everything that 'might be useful'; consent bundled; default settings inherited from vendor templates
maturity.mature: Mandatory vs optional fields distinguished and justified; opt-in for non-essential; purpose-specific consent implemented; periodic field-necessity reviews
maturity.advanced: Automated data minimisation lints at collection forms; purpose-isolated data stores enforced by access control; pseudonymisation pipelines for analytics; default-privacy A/B testing

toolExamples: Data minimisation linters in form builders
controls.controlObjective: Ensure default settings on all systems and processes processing personal data are the most privacy-protective option, and that the volume of personal data collected, used, retained, and disclosed is the minimum necessary for the specified purpose.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Forms differentiate mandatory and optional fields with justification for each mandatory field. Cookie banners default to strictly-necessary only; secondary cookies require affirmative opt-in. Profile visibility, data sharing, and notification preferences default to most-private setting. Pseudonymisation applied to analytics datasets. Periodic field-necessity reviews remove fields no longer needed. Consent is granular and specific per purpose.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Excessive collection beyond what is necessary breaches the General Principle (s6). Bundled consent fails consent validity tests. Default-public profile settings lead to inadvertent disclosure. Unjustified retention of optional fields creates breach exposure and complicates DSARs.
controls.samplingGuidance: Sample 5 active data collection forms (web, app, paper) and verify (1) mandatory fields are justified, (2) optional fields are clearly marked, (3) consent is unbundled where required, (4) default cookie state is privacy-protective. Inspect default profile/sharing settings on the customer-facing platform. Review the last data field necessity review and confirm redundant fields were deleted.

controls.name: Children's Data Protection by Design
controls.description: ISSUED REQUIREMENTS (per JPDP DPbD Guideline April 2026 §5.3, §10.2, §11.2): valid parental consent for under-18 subjects; correction mechanism easily usable by parents/guardians; access mechanism easily usable by parents/guardians. Other elements (best-interests-of-child, age verification mechanisms, anti-lockout, profiling/marketing prohibition) are PCP-derived leading practice — NOT in the issued JPDP guideline.
keyActivities: [ISSUED §5.3] Where the data subject is under 18, obtain valid consent on behalf of the subject from the parent, guardian, or person with parental responsibility
[ISSUED §10.2] Ensure correction mechanisms are easily usable by parents/guardians for under-18 subjects
[ISSUED §11.2] Ensure access mechanisms are easily usable by parents/guardians for under-18 subjects
[LEADING PRACTICE] Identify whether the product/service is directed at, intended for, or likely to be accessed by children (in reality)
[LEADING PRACTICE] Implement age verification mechanism proportionate to processing risk
[LEADING PRACTICE] Apply strictest privacy defaults to all child-accessible services
[LEADING PRACTICE] Use clear, concise, child-friendly language in notices and consent flows
[LEADING PRACTICE] Where appropriate, provide a parental dashboard with activity history and settings

[LEADING PRACTICE] Where engaging in profiling or ADM affecting children, document a best-interests-of-child analysis. Note: a strict prohibition on profiling/marketing of children was in PCP §2.57 but did NOT survive into either the issued DPbD or ADMP Guidelines

maturity.basic: Generic notice for all users; age field with self-declaration; no special handling for children
maturity.mature: Age verification proportionate to risk; child-friendly notice variant; parental consent for under-18 with email confirmation; rights flows include child-appropriate language
maturity.advanced: Risk-based age verification (digital ID, biometric with anti-spoofing for high-risk); parental dashboard with full activity history; automated detection of likely-child users; profiling/ADM disabled by default for child accounts; best-interests assessment built into product approval

toolExamples: Custom child-account flag in user model
relatedStandards: UN Convention on the Rights of the Child Article 3 (best interests) — analyst reference, NOT cited in issued JPDP guideline
controls.controlObjective: Ensure processing of personal data of subjects under 18 meets the issued §5.3, §10.2, §11.2 requirements (parental consent; accessible parent/guardian correction and access mechanisms). Where leading-practice elements apply (age verification, child-friendly notice, profiling-best-interests analysis), document them as leading practice — not as JPDP-issued requirements.
