controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Issued requirements: valid parental/guardian consent for under-18 subjects (§5.3); correction mechanism accessible to parents/guardians (§10.2); access mechanism accessible to parents/guardians (§11.2). Leading practice: service identifies whether children are likely accessing it; age verification proportionate to risk; child-friendly notice variant; best-interests analysis documented for any profiling/ADM affecting children.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Processing of children's data without parental consent (s40 Issued §5.3 breach). Inaccessible correction/access mechanisms breach §10.2/§11.2. Where the service profiles or applies ADM to children without best-interests analysis, this is a leading-practice gap (no longer a Malaysian regulatory prohibition since PCP §2.57 was dropped); GDPR Recital 38 / EU DSA / UK ICO Children's Code may still apply for cross-jurisdictional services.
controls.samplingGuidance: Identify whether the service has under-18 subjects. If yes: verify parental/guardian consent obtained; verify correction and access mechanisms are usable by parents/guardians (test with sample requests). Inspect any profiling or ADM operation affecting children — document best-interests analysis as leading practice. Note: the JPDP DPbD Guideline does NOT prohibit profiling of children for advertising; framing must be cross-jurisdictional best practice, not a JPDP citation.

controls.name: ADMP Trigger Identification and Scoping
controls.description: Identify all processing operations involving automated decision-making and profiling, assess which meet the Ambang ADMP (legal effects or significantly affects data subjects), and document the basis on which each operation is in or out of scope of the issued JPDP ADMP Guideline.
keyActivities: Inventory all processing operations involving fully or partially automated decisions or profiling (issued §3.2 confirms partial automation in scope)
For each, assess: in scope? legal effects (kesan undang-undang)? significantly affects (kesan signifikan)?
Engage DPO from the earliest stage of any ADMP design (§5.1)
Document scoping decisions with rationale, signed off by DPO
Trigger DPIA for any in-scope ADMP regardless of subject count (§6.1) — quantitative >10,000 cross-check is supplementary, not gating
Maintain an ADM register: name, purpose, model/algorithm, in-scope status, basis, lawful ground, exception/exemption claimed
Review register quarterly and on every model or process change

maturity.basic: Ad-hoc identification; no formal register
maturity.mature: Formal ADM register; scoping criteria documented; DPO sign-off; quarterly review
maturity.advanced: Automated discovery from system telemetry; integration with model registry; change-triggered re-scoping; ADM register linked to RoPA

toolExamples: Custom ADM register in GRC platform
controls.controlObjective: Ensure all automated decision-making and profiling operations are inventoried, assessed against the regulatory trigger (legal effects or significant effects), and scoped accurately so that ADM Restrictions are applied where required.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: ADM register exists and is current. Each in-scope operation has documented scoping decision (Ambang ADMP assessment), basis, and any Exception/Exemption claimed. DPO engaged from earliest design stage with sign-off recorded. DPIA performed for every in-scope ADMP regardless of subject count. Cross-link to RoPA. Register reviewed quarterly and on every material change.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: In-scope ADMP operates unidentified, with no s7 notice and no s38 withdrawal pathway where consent is the basis. DPIA missed despite §6.1 mandate. Commissioner enforcement on a triggered process the controller never recognised. Discriminatory outcomes operate without DPO oversight.
controls.samplingGuidance: Obtain the ADM register. Cross-reference against system inventory and RoPA — verify completeness. Sample 5 entries and verify scoping rationale, lawful basis, and exception/exemption claim. Inspect the most recent quarterly review. Walk through 1 process not on the register that uses ML/AI to confirm exclusion is justified.

controls.name: ADMP Transparency and Right to Information
controls.description: Provide data subjects with notice that ADM/profiling is occurring (mandatory under s7 + §9.3) and, on a permissive basis ('boleh', §9.4), explanation of logic, reasons, and likely impact. The issued guideline explicitly allows withholding of confidential information, trade secrets, IP, or proprietary rights from any explanation. The PCP doctrine that the Right to Information is 'retained even where Exceptions apply' was NOT carried into the issued text.
keyActivities: [MANDATORY] Update privacy notice(s) to disclose the FACT that ADM/profiling is involved for every in-scope operation
[PERMISSIVE] Where the controller chooses to provide explanation: describe the type of decisions, the reasons, and the likely impact (§9.4)
[PERMISSIVE] Document trade-secret / IP / confidentiality redactions where they apply
Provide just-in-time notice at the moment of an automated decision (e.g., loan application form, credit decision page, hiring portal)
Maintain version history of ADM disclosures
Train customer-facing staff to explain the automated decision in plain language where the controller has elected to disclose

maturity.basic: Generic mention of 'automated processing' in privacy notice
maturity.mature: Specific disclosure per ADM operation with meaningful logic; just-in-time notice at decision points
maturity.advanced: Per-decision explainability — every individual decision is accompanied by a personalised explanation referencing inputs and impact; integrated with model explainability tools

controls.controlObjective: Mandatorily disclose the FACT of ADM/profiling for every in-scope operation per s7 + §9.3. Where the controller chooses to provide explanation of logic, reasons, and likely impact, do so per §9.4 with documented trade-secret/IP carve-outs. Note: PCP doctrine that the Right to Information is retained under all Exceptions did NOT carry into the issued text.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Privacy notice lists each in-scope ADM operation with the FACT that ADM/profiling is involved (mandatory). Where the controller has elected to explain: meaningful logic, reasons, and likely impact are disclosed (permissive); trade-secret/IP redactions are documented. Just-in-time notice appears at the decision moment. Customer service can explain a specific decision in plain language where the controller has chosen to disclose. Disclosures version-controlled.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Data subjects unaware that ADM/profiling is involved (breach of s7 + §9.3). Commissioner finding of breach of Notice and Choice principle. Reputational harm from opacity. Where the controller does provide explanation, inconsistent or trade-secret-claiming carve-outs without documentation invite challenge.
controls.samplingGuidance: Review the privacy notice for ADM disclosures. For each in-scope operation in the ADM register, verify a corresponding disclosure of the FACT of ADM exists (mandatory). Sample 3 just-in-time notices in production user flows. Where the controller has elected to provide explanation, verify customer service can explain a sample decision in plain language. Where carve-outs are claimed, verify they are documented (which carve-out, why).

controls.name: ADMP Human Review Mechanism (Cross-jurisdictional Best Practice)
controls.description: [NOT IN ISSUED JPDP GUIDELINE — LEADING PRACTICE] Provide a human review pathway for automated decisions with reviewer authority to override and documented justification. The PCP §2.13(c) Right to Human Review and 21/14-day SLA were NOT carried into the April 2026 issued ADMP Guideline. Retained as cross-jurisdictional best practice (GDPR Article 22(3); UK ICO; AI-Governance leading practice). Findings should NOT cite the JPDP ADMP Guideline for absence of human review.
controls.regulatorySource: Cross-jurisdictional best practice — not in the April 2026 issued ADMP Guideline. The PCP §2.13(c) proposal was dropped.
keyActivities: Build the review request mechanism into the same system that produces the automated decision (not a separate manual process)
Assign trained reviewers with authority to overturn the recommendation
Train reviewers in the model's logic so they can meaningfully override (not rubber-stamp)
Define an internal review SLA consistent with general data subject rights timelines under Act 709 — note: the JPDP ADMP Guideline does NOT impose a specific SLA
Maintain a review log: original decision, reviewer, additional information considered, outcome, justification
Sample-test reviewers' override rate; investigate near-zero override rates
Provide reasons in writing where the data subject requests them (cross-jurisdictional best practice)

maturity.basic: Review on request goes to general support inbox; no formal SLA
maturity.mature: Defined SLA, trained reviewers, override authority, review log
maturity.advanced: Automated routing to qualified reviewers; reviewer training tracked; periodic calibration sessions to align overrides; statistical monitoring of reviewer-decision agreement to detect rubber-stamping

controls.controlObjective: Provide a substantive human review of automated decisions on request as a leading practice — qualified reviewers, defined internal timelines, written justification, and meaningful override authority. The JPDP ADMP Guideline does NOT impose human review; this control aligns with international best practice for AI accountability.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Where the controller offers human review (best practice): mechanism is integrated with the decision system; reviewers are named, trained, and qualified; internal SLA defined and met; review log captures original decision, additional information, reviewer outcome with justification; reviewer override rate is non-zero and analysed.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: If offered, reviews that are rubber-stamped or where reviewers lack authority/competence undermine the leading-practice value. NOTE: absence of human review itself is NOT a breach of the JPDP ADMP Guideline — the issued guideline does not impose human review. Findings citing absence should reference best practice or AI Governance frameworks, not the JPDP ADMP Guideline.
controls.samplingGuidance: Where the controller offers human review: sample 10 review requests from the last 12 months; verify internal SLA met, reviewer was qualified, review log complete, justification documented. Compute the reviewer override rate. Where the controller does NOT offer human review: do not issue a finding citing the JPDP ADMP Guideline; cross-reference the AI Governance repo for related leading-practice controls.

controls.name: ADMP Withdrawal of Consent and Alternative Pathways (s38 anchored)
controls.description: Where consent is the lawful basis for ADMP, provide a Section 38 withdrawal of consent mechanism that is as easy to use as giving consent (per general s38 obligation). The issued JPDP ADMP Guideline does NOT codify a standalone Right to Refuse beyond s38 withdrawal. Where contract necessity or legal compliance is the basis, no refusal right is created by the ADMP Guideline.
keyActivities: Provide an s38 withdrawal mechanism at the decision point and in the privacy notice (where consent is the basis)
Where withdrawal is exercised: route the request to a non-automated path (where business permits) or cease processing
Document withdrawal events: who withdrew, what processing, what occurred next

Where Exceptions are claimed (§9.9.1 contract necessity / §9.9.2 legal compliance / §9.9.3 consent — note plain consent, NOT explicit consent): document the claimed exception and verify the conditions are met
For sensitive personal data ADMP: process only under s40 conditions — explicit consent (s40(1)(a)) OR s40(1)(b) grounds (employment law / vital interests / medical / legal proceedings)
Per §8.2: 'boleh mempertimbangkan' (may consider) implementing technical (encryption, access controls) and organisational safeguards for sensitive PD ADMP — permissive

maturity.basic: Refusal handled ad-hoc via customer service; no formal alternative path
maturity.mature: Refusal mechanism exposed at decision point; non-automated path defined and operational
maturity.advanced: Refusal triggers automatic routing to alternative processing; refusal log integrated with consent platform; Exception claims peer-reviewed before invocation

toolExamples: Consent management platforms with refusal hooks
Workflow tools with non-automated lane (Camunda, Pega)
controls.controlObjective: Where consent is the lawful basis for ADMP, provide an effective Section 38 withdrawal of consent mechanism. Document any §9.9 Exception claimed (contract necessity / legal compliance / consent — note plain consent, NOT explicit). For sensitive PD, ensure s40 conditions are met.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Section 38 withdrawal mechanism visible at the decision point and in the privacy notice (where consent is the basis). Non-automated alternative path operates where business permits. Withdrawal events logged with outcome. §9.9 Exception claims documented per operation, peer-reviewed, and revisited if circumstances change. Sensitive PD ADMP operates only under s40(1)(a) explicit consent or s40(1)(b) ground.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: s38 withdrawal mechanism missing or harder than giving consent — direct breach. §9.9 Exception claims invalid or stale (e.g., contract no longer requires ADMP, statute amended). Sensitive PD ADMP without s40 lawful basis — direct breach of s40.
controls.samplingGuidance: Inspect the s38 withdrawal mechanism in the user flow at the decision point and confirm 'as easy as giving consent'. Sample 5 withdrawal events from last 12 months; verify outcome. Review §9.9 Exception claims for 3 in-scope operations; verify conditions are met (signed contract requiring ADMP / statutory citation / valid consent under §9.9.3 — note plain consent suffices for §9.9.3). For any sensitive-data ADMP, verify s40 lawful condition is documented.

controls.name: ADMP Bias Monitoring and AI/Generative AI Safeguards
controls.description: Monitor automated decisions and profiling for accuracy, bias, and discriminatory outcomes, and apply the 7 AI best practices in §10.2 of the issued ADMP Guideline. Note: §10.2 verbs are permissive ('boleh menerapkan' — may apply); these are best practices, not mandatory obligations. PCP §2.25(b) 'no manipulation' safeguard was DROPPED from the issued text.
keyActivities: Monitor model accuracy and bias on a defined cadence (e.g., quarterly or per major model update)
Track output disparities across protected attributes (race, gender, age, disability) where lawful and applicable
[§10.2.1] Pre-deployment AI risk assessment — weigh commercial objectives against risks
[§10.2.2] Use AI respecting dignity; maintain accuracy; recognise AI limitations; do not use for inappropriate purposes
[§10.2.3 NEW] Disclose AI use in the privacy notice (scoped to be useful, not overly long or technical)
[§10.2.4] Mitigate over-reliance / automation bias
[§10.2.5] Operator training on AI system logic and limitations
[§10.2.6] AI not the only factor in policy or individual decisions
[§10.2.7] Trained reviewers (proactive, purposeful, authoritative, competent)
Maintain a model card or system documentation: version, training data summary, accuracy metrics, known limitations, intended use
Test against synthetic adversarial inputs and edge cases

maturity.basic: Accuracy reviewed at deployment; no ongoing bias testing
maturity.mature: Quarterly accuracy and bias review; remediation tracked; operator training records; model cards maintained
maturity.advanced: Continuous bias monitoring with alerting; A/B testing for fairness improvements; adversarial testing and red-team exercises; fairness scorecards reported to senior management

controls.controlObjective: Continuously monitor automated decisions and profiling for accuracy, bias, and discriminatory outcomes, and apply the 7 AI best practices in issued ADMP §10.2 — recognising the permissive 'boleh' framing. Notice disclosure of AI use (§10.2.3) is best practice and aligns with the s7 mandatory FACT-of-ADM disclosure under §9.3.
controls.whatGoodLooksLike: Bias and accuracy metrics monitored on a defined cadence. Disparities investigated and remediated. Pre-deployment AI risk assessment documented (§10.2.1). Operators trained on AI logic (§10.2.5). AI not the only factor in policy decisions (§10.2.6). Trained reviewers in place (§10.2.7). Model cards documented. AI use disclosed in privacy notice (§10.2.3). Adversarial and edge-case testing conducted.
controls.keyRiskIfAbsent: Discriminatory outcomes (HR, credit, healthcare). Inaccurate predictions causing real harm. Automation bias suppresses human override. NOTE: §10.2 best practices are permissive — absence is a leading-practice gap, not a strict §10.2 breach. The §9.3 disclosure of FACT of ADM (mandatory) is a separate strict obligation; failure breaches s7 directly.
controls.samplingGuidance: Obtain the most recent bias/accuracy report for an in-scope ADM operation. Verify cadence aligns with policy. Inspect remediation actions for prior findings. Review model cards for 3 systems. Confirm operator training records. For AI deployments, verify pre-deployment risk assessment exists. Verify privacy notice discloses AI use. NOTE: do not cite §10.2 as a strict obligation — the verbs are permissive.
